diagnosis: You are missing one or more teeth and a dental implant — a titanium post surgically placed into the jawbone — has been recommended to replace the missing tooth root and restore your smile and chewing function.
treatment_steps: Take a 3D X-ray (CBCT) to evaluate bone quality, quantity, and important anatomical structures
Review your medical history and medications for any factors that may affect healing
Numb the area completely with local anesthesia; sedation options may be available
Make a small incision in the gum to expose the bone where the implant will be placed
Gradually drill precise osteotomy holes into the jawbone at the exact position and angle
Screw the titanium implant post into the bone — this acts as the artificial tooth root
Suture the gum tissue closed over or around the implant
Allow several months for the bone to fuse with the implant (osseointegration)
Place an abutment (connector) and take impressions for the final crown
Attach the custom-made permanent crown, bridge, or denture
benefits: Looks and feels like a natural tooth
Prevents bone loss in the jaw that normally occurs after tooth loss
Does not require filing down adjacent healthy teeth (unlike a bridge)
Permanent solution that can last decades with proper care
Restores natural chewing function and clear speech
Easy to maintain — brush and floss like normal teeth
risks: Bleeding, swelling, bruising, and discomfort for several days after surgery
Temporary or, rarely, permanent numbness, tingling, or change in sensation of the lip, chin, cheek, gums, tongue, or throat due to nerve involvement
Damage to adjacent teeth or blood vessels during surgery
Implant failure to integrate with the bone, requiring removal and possible repeat surgery
Loosening or fracture of the implant, abutment, or crown over time
Sinus complications for implants in the upper back jaw, which may require a sinus lift procedure
Gum recession around the implant affecting appearance
Prolonged or incomplete healing, especially in patients who smoke or have uncontrolled diabetes
Long-term reduced effectiveness of birth-control pills if certain antibiotics are prescribed after surgery
Patients taking bisphosphonate medications (for osteoporosis) or those who have had radiation therapy to the jaw may have an increased risk of poor healing or bone necrosis
Additional procedures may be needed during surgery, such as bone grafting or sinus lifts
alternatives: Fixed dental bridge (uses adjacent teeth for support)
No treatment (the gap will remain, which may cause nearby teeth to shift and chewing function to decrease)
Resin-bonded bridge (Maryland bridge) in select cases
risks_of_declining: If you choose not to replace the missing tooth, neighboring teeth may gradually tilt and shift into the gap. The opposing tooth may also over-erupt. This can change your bite and lead to jaw problems, difficulty chewing, and increased risk of decay and gum disease. Bone loss at the extraction site will continue over time.
prognosis: Dental implants have a high success rate, typically reported above 95 percent for healthy patients. Success depends on good oral hygiene, regular dental visits, avoiding smoking, and following post-operative instructions. No outcome is guaranteed and individual results may vary. Implants can fail years later due to gum disease, overload, or medical factors.
